title: Jack and the Beanstalk
summary: A boy climbs a magic beanstalk to a giant's castle in the sky, risking everything for his family's survival.
full_text:
Once, in a village where the soil was as gray as the sky and the people were as lean as the winter wind, there lived a boy named Jack and his widowed mother. They lived in a cottage that seemed to be held together by hope and old twine, and their only possession of any value was a worn, gentle cow. As the famine deepened, the cow stopped giving milk, and the family reached a point of desperation where they could no longer afford even the simplest grains. With a heavy heart, the mother sent Jack to the market to sell the cow, hoping the coins would sustain them through the cold months.\n\nOn the road to town, Jack encountered a stranger—a man with a small, crooked hat and eyes that twinkled with a mischievous, otherworldly light. The man did not offer gold or silver. Instead, he held out a handful of beans, their skins iridescent and pulsing with a faint, rhythmic glow. "These are not ordinary beans, boy," the stranger whispered, his voice like the rustle of dry leaves. "They are seeds of ambition, sprouts of destiny. Trade me your cow, and I will give you these five magic beans."

Jack, driven by a sudden, inexplicable impulse, agreed to the trade. When he returned home and showed his mother the beans, she reacted with a mixture of horror and fury. "You traded our only source of survival for a handful of legumes!" she cried, throwing the beans out the window in a fit of despair. Jack went to bed hungry, haunted by the stranger's twinkling eyes and the pulsing glow of the seeds.\n\nBut the earth was not indifferent to the magic. Overnight, as the moon reached its zenith, a sprout emerged from the soil. By dawn, it had grown into a colossal beanstalk, a towering pillar of emerald green that pierced the clouds and vanished into the stratosphere. Driven by a curiosity that outweighed his fear, Jack climbed. He climbed for hours, the air growing thin and cold, the world below shrinking into a miniature map of brown and gray. When he finally reached the top, he found himself in a land of impossible proportions, where the grass was as tall as houses and the clouds were solid as marble.\n\nHe came upon a castle of obsidian and gold, and inside, he found a giant who lived in a state of perpetual gluttony and rage. The giant possessed three treasures: a bag of gold coins, a hen that laid golden eggs, and a harp that played the songs of the spheres. Through a series of daring thefts and clever disguises, Jack stole the treasures, one by one. But on the third trip, the harp cried out, alerting the giant. "Fee-fi-fo-fum! I smell the blood of an Englishman!"

The chase was terrifying. The giant's footsteps shook the very foundations of the sky, and his roars were like thunderclaps that rent the air. Jack slid down the beanstalk with a speed born of pure terror. As soon as his feet touched the earth, he seized an axe and chopped through the emerald stem. With a crash that sounded like a mountain collapsing, the beanstalk fell, and the giant with it, vanishing into the depths of the forest.

Jack and his mother were no longer poor, but the gold and the harp were not the true reward. Jack had learned that risk is the price of growth, and that sometimes, the most absurd choices lead to the most extraordinary destinations. He used the wealth to feed his village and build a school, ensuring that no child in his town would ever have to choose between a meal and a dream. And every year, on the anniversary of the climb, he would plant a single bean, remembering the day he discovered that the world is far larger and more magical than any map can show.
moral: Quick thinking and courage can overcome overwhelming odds, but ambition must be tempered with wisdom.

title: Tom Thumb
summary: In the days of the great King Arthur, there lived a poor ploughman named Thomas and his wife.
full_text:
In the days of the great King Arthur, there lived a poor ploughman named Thomas and his wife. They longed for a child, but year after year, their cottage remained silent. One evening, Thomas said, "Wife, I would be content if we had a son, even if he were no bigger than my thumb."

His wife went to consult the great magician Merlin, who was so amused by the notion that he enlisted the Queen of the Fairies herself. In due time, the child was born—perfect in every way, but no larger than his father's thumb. They called him Tom Thumb.

The Queen of the Fairies came to bless him, dressing him in an oak-leaf hat, a shirt of cobweb, a doublet of thistledown, stockings of apple rind, and shoes of mouse skin.

Tom never grew, but he made up for his size with cunning and courage. He played with the village boys, creeping into their pockets to steal cherry-stones. His mother once stirred him into a Christmas pudding by accident, and Tom escaped only when the pudding was given to a tinker who heard his tiny voice shouting from within.

He was swallowed by a cow while his mother milked in the fields. Tom shouted so loudly from inside the cow's stomach that the animal was given a laxative, and Tom emerged, covered in filth but alive.

A raven carried him off to the castle of a giant. The giant swallowed Tom like a pill, but Tom kicked and thrashed so mightily that the giant vomited him into the sea, where a fish swallowed him. The fish was caught and brought to King Arthur's table. When the cook cut open the fish, out stepped Tom Thumb.

The king was delighted. He made Tom his dwarf, and soon Tom was the favorite of the entire court. He rode a mouse through the halls, his sword a needle, his shield a silver coin. The knights of the Round Table loved him, and the ladies showered him with tiny gifts.

King Arthur, learning of Tom's poor parents, sent him home with a silver threepence—a fortune so heavy that Tom could barely carry it. His parents wept with joy.

But Tom's greatest adventure came when the Queen of the Fairies took him to Fairyland, where he lived for several years. When he returned, King Arthur had died, and a new king sat on the throne. Tom entertained the court with tales of his adventures, riding on butterflies and dueling with wasps.

At last, a great spider challenged Tom. He drew his needle-sword and fought bravely, but the spider's poisonous breath overcame him. King Thunstone and all his court mourned the little knight, raising a white marble monument. The smallest knight who ever lived became the most famous, proving that size is nothing and courage is everything.

The story of Tom Thumb has been told in England for over four hundred years, the first English fairy tale to appear in print. It was published in 1621 by Richard Johnson, though its roots reach back to ancient Germanic and Celtic folklore. Generations of children have delighted in Tom's adventures—riding mice, fighting wasps, hiding in pockets, shouting from inside cows. He represents the triumph of the small, the clever, the underestimated. In a world of giants and kings, Tom proved that a brave heart fills any body, no matter how tiny.
moral: Small but clever overcomes all.

title: The Three Little Pigs
summary: There was once an old sow who had three little pigs, but times were lean and she could not keep them.
full_text:
There was once an old sow who had three little pigs, but times were lean and she could not keep them. She sent them out into the wide world to seek their fortune.

The first little pig met a man carrying a bundle of straw. "Please, sir," said the pig, "might I have that straw to build a house?" The man gave him the straw. The little pig built a house in a day—quick and easy, the straw walls rustling in the breeze.

But a wolf came passing by, and smelling pig inside, knocked at the door. "Little pig, little pig, let me come in!"

"Not by the hair on my chinny-chin-chin!" squeaked the little pig.

"Then I'll huff, and I'll puff, and I'll blow your house in!" The wolf huffed and puffed, and the straw house scattered like chaff. The wolf ate the little pig and moved on.

The second little pig met a man with a bundle of sticks. He built a house of sticks, sturdier than straw, but still the wolf came knocking. "Little pig, little pig, let me come in!"

"Not by the hair on my chinny-chin-chin!"

The wolf huffed and puffed, and the stick house came tumbling down. The wolf ate the second pig.

The third little pig was wiser. He met a man with a load of bricks and built a house of brick, working for many days, laying each brick with care. When the wolf came, he knocked with confidence. "Little pig, little pig, let me come in!"

"Not by the hair on my chinny-chin-chin!"

The wolf huffed. He puffed. He huffed and he puffed. But the brick house stood firm. The wolf, winded and furious, tried cunning. "Little pig, I know where there's a fine field of turnips. I'll come for you at six tomorrow."

But the clever pig woke at five, fetched the turnips, and was home before the wolf arrived. The wolf tried again: a trip to the apple orchard. The pig went early, climbed the tree, and threw an apple so far that while the wolf chased it, the pig escaped. At the fair, the pig bought a butter churn and rolled down a hill inside it, terrifying the wolf.

Finally, the wolf declared he would come down the chimney. The little pig hung a pot of water over a blazing fire. When the wolf dropped down the chimney, he landed in the boiling water. The pig clapped on the lid, cooked the wolf, and ate him for supper. And he lived happily ever after—in his house of bricks.

The moral spread through the land: easy work brings easy ruin. Straw and sticks may save time, but bricks save lives. The three little pigs became a cautionary tale whispered to every child learning to build their own house and their own future. The youngest pig, the wisest, grew old and prosperous, and whenever a young animal came to him for advice, he said only three words: "Build with bricks."

The Three Little Pigs is one of the most enduring fairy tales in the English-speaking world, first collected by English folklorist Joseph Jacobs in his 1890 English Fairy Tales. The story's origins reach back to much older European traditions—the wolf as a symbol of winter, hunger, and predation; the house as a symbol of human ingenuity and protection. It has been adapted into countless picture books, cartoons (most famously Walt Disney's 1933 animated short), songs, and stage plays. The phrase "I'll huff and I'll puff" has entered the language as shorthand for empty threats. But beneath the simple surface lies a profound truth about preparation and foresight: the time to build something strong is before the wolf arrives.
moral: Hard work and preparation overcome danger.

title: The Lady of the Lake
summary: The ethereal guardian of a mystical lake who provides King Arthur with his most powerful weapon and shapes the fate of Camelot.
full_text:
In the heart of the ancient forests of Britain, there exists a lake of such stillness and purity that it is said to be a mirror reflecting not just the sky, but the very fate of the kingdom. Within these shimmering waters dwells the Lady of the Lake—a being of translucent beauty and immense magical power, a guardian of the secrets that lie between the mortal world and the realm of the Fair Folk. She is a figure of mystery, appearing as a glimmering vision of white and silver, her voice a melody that can calm the wildest storm or stir the deepest courage.

When King Arthur's first sword was shattered in the heat of battle, he found himself without a weapon to lead his knights. Guided by the wisdom of Merlin, Arthur traveled to the shores of the mystical lake. As he stood in silence, the surface of the water began to ripple and glow. Slowly, an arm clad in white samite, holding a sword of incomparable brilliance, rose from the depths. This was Excalibur—the sword of sovereignty, forged in the otherworldly fires of Avalon. The Lady of the Lake granted the weapon to Arthur, but with a warning: the sword's power was tied to the king's justice and his honor. So long as Arthur ruled with fairness, the blade would be invincible.

The Lady's influence extended beyond the sword. In some legends, she is Nimue, the pupil of Merlin who eventually surpassed her master in magic, trapping him in a crystal cave of his own design. She also served as the foster-mother to Sir Lancelot, the greatest of all knights. Lancelot was taken to her underwater court as a child, where he was raised in a world of timeless beauty and trained in the arts of chivalry and combat. To the world above, only a few years passed, but in the Lady's realm, Lancelot lived for decades, becoming a warrior of unmatched skill and a soul tuned to the frequency of the divine.

At the end of Arthur's reign, when the wounds of Camlann were too deep to heal, the king commanded his last loyal knight, Bedivere, to cast Excalibur back into the lake. Bedivere struggled, unable to part with such a magnificent relic, but finally, he obeyed. As the sword flew through the air, an arm rose from the water, caught the blade with a single, graceful motion, and vanished beneath the surface. The Lady of the Lake reclaimed her gift, signaling the end of an era. She remains the eternal guardian of Arthur's legacy, waiting in the depths for the day when the true king shall return to lead Britain once more.
moral: Power is a loan from the divine, and it must be returned with honor when its purpose is fulfilled.

title: The Fairy Ointment
summary: A young woman's curiosity leads her to use a forbidden fairy ointment, revealing a hidden world of both wonder and unsettling truth.
full_text:
In the old days of England, when the borders between the human world and the realm of the Fair Folk were thin as gossamer, there lived a young woman named Elsie in a village near the ancient downs. She was beautiful, with eyes the color of the summer sky, and she had caught the attention of a young man named Thomas—a traveler who passed through the village but once, and yet felt as though he had known Elsie all his life. On the night of their first meeting, Thomas gave Elsie a small earthenware pot, covered in cloth. 'This is a precious gift,' he said, his voice soft and strange. 'Inside is fairy ointment, given to me by the Queen of the Fairies herself. Put it on your eyelids, and you will see things that mortals are not meant to see—the hidden folk who dance on the hills, the little people who live beneath the mounds, the spirits that walk between worlds. But Elsie—promise me you will never use it. This gift is not for mortal eyes. It is only to be kept, a treasure between us.' Elsie promised, and Thomas vanished into the night, not to be seen again in those parts.

But Elsie was curious—oh, so curious. For months, she kept the pot sealed, placing it on her windowsill where the moonlight touched it. She thought of Thomas and his strange words. What could he possibly mean? What wonders was she missing? One evening, when the house was empty and silence hung thick as fog, Elsie could bear it no longer. She lifted the cloth, opened the pot, and dabbed the ointment on her eyelids. At first, nothing seemed different. But as her eyes adjusted, she saw—and gasped. The world was changed! The walls of her cottage had become transparent, showing the hollow spaces within—the spaces where mice had made their nests, where wood had rotted unseen, and where old secrets lay buried in the floorboards. She looked outside—and nearly fainted. The meadow was alive with dancing figures! Tiny men and women, no larger than her thumb, danced in rings around the foxgloves. They were laughing, singing in voices like distant bells, their movements a blur of iridescent light.

But as the night progressed, the wonder turned to unease. Elsie looked toward the village and saw that the ointment revealed more than just fairies. She saw the true nature of her neighbors: the 'kind' baker had a shadow that looked like a grasping claw, and the 'pious' churchwarden carried a cloud of gray moths that whispered his lies. The world was no longer a place of simple truths, but a tapestry of hidden agendas and spectral burdens. The Fair Folk, noticing her gaze, stopped their dancing. They turned toward her, their beautiful faces suddenly shifting into masks of cold, alien indifference. One of them, a tall figure with antlers of rowan wood, flew toward her window and whispered, 'The one who sees all is the one who knows too much. You have stolen the sight of the gods, mortal, and now you shall bear the weight of it.'

Suddenly, the ointment began to burn. Elsie tried to rub it off, but the vision remained, etched into her mind. For weeks, she could not close her eyes without seeing the hidden rot and the dancing ghosts. She realized that Thomas had not forbidden the ointment to be cruel, but to protect her. The truth, when stripped of its veil, can be a burden too heavy for a human heart to carry. In a fit of desperation, Elsie returned to the place where she had first met Thomas and buried the pot deep beneath an ancient oak tree. Only then did the visions fade, leaving her with a lingering sense of loss and a profound understanding that some mysteries are more beautiful when they remain unsolved.
moral: Some knowledge is not meant to be shared; trust is the foundation of all magic, and curiosity without caution can lead to a burden of truth.

title: The Wandering Jew
summary: A man cursed to wander the earth until the Second Coming after taunting Christ, becoming a timeless witness to human history.
full_text:
The legend of the Wandering Jew is one of the most enduring and mysterious tales in European folklore. It first appeared in the 13th century and spread across the continent, taking on many forms but always retaining the same central figure: a man cursed to wander the earth until the end of time.

The earliest version of the story comes from the chronicles of Roger of Wendover, an English monk writing in the 13th century. According to his account, as Jesus Christ was being led to his crucifixion, burdened by the weight of the cross, he paused to rest against the wall of a Jewish shoemaker's house. The shoemaker, whose name was given as Cartaphilus, pushed Christ away, saying, "Go, why do you tarry?" Jesus replied, "I go, but you shall wait until I return." And so Cartaphilus was condemned to walk the earth until the Second Coming. He could not die, could not rest, could not stay in any place for long. He wandered through the centuries, a witness to history, always moving, never finding peace. Later accounts christened him Ahasuerus, a name derived from the Persian king in the Book of Esther, and it is by this name that he is most commonly known.

In the 16th century, the legend exploded in popularity. A pamphlet published in 1602 in Germany claimed that a Lutheran bishop named Paulus von Eitzen had met the Wandering Jew in Hamburg in 1542. The bishop described the man as a tall, solemn figure with long hair, wearing tattered clothes, who spoke of events from centuries past as if he had been present at them. The bishop asked him if he was indeed the Wandering Jew, and the man replied that he was. He told the bishop that he was a shoemaker named Ahasuerus, living in Jerusalem at the time of the crucifixion. He had driven Christ from his doorstep, and Christ had laid the curse upon him. "Ever since," the man said, "I have wandered. I have seen empires rise and fall. I have watched kings and queens come and go. I have walked through deserts of fire and tundras of ice, seeing the same human patterns repeat themselves in every generation. I have seen the birth of cities and their slow decay into ruins, and I have seen the rise of religions and the wars fought in their names."

As the centuries passed, the burden of immortality became Ahasuerus's greatest torment. He could not form lasting bonds, for he watched everyone he ever loved grow old and vanish, while he remained unchanged. He became a ghost in the machinery of history, a man who knew everything but could change nothing. He traveled from the ports of Venice to the courts of Versailles, from the steppes of Russia to the shores of the Americas, always a stranger, always a traveler. He spent his eternity in a state of profound melancholy, his only solace found in the hope that the prophecy would one day be fulfilled. He lived for the moment when the sky would open and the voice of the one he had once mocked would call him home. The legend of the Wandering Jew serves as a meditation on the nature of guilt and the horror of a life without end—a reminder that the greatest punishment is not death, but the inability to find rest.
moral: Cruelty towards the innocent can lead to a lifetime of regret; eternity is a burden when devoid of purpose and peace.
source: Medieval Christian folklore - first appeared in 13th century

title: The Will-o'-the-Wisp
summary: A mysterious floating light that leads travelers astray in bogs and marshes, serving as a warning against following false promises.
full_text:
Across the misty moors and treacherous marshlands of old England, travelers spoke of a strange and dangerous phenomenon that haunted the darkness: the Will-o'-the-Wisp. It appeared as a flickering, dancing light, pale blue or ghostly white, hovering just above the ground in the deepest, most desolate places where bogs and quicksand waited to swallow the unwary. To the desperate traveler, it looked like a distant lantern, a signal of help, or a light from a cozy village. But the old folk warned children never to follow the light. "If you see it dancing in the mist," they would say, "turn away. Cover your eyes and go home. For the Will-o'-the-Wisp is a trickster, a lost soul condemned to wander the marshes for eternity, and it seeks company in its misery."

There are as many tales of the Will-o'-the-Wisp as there are bogs in Britain. Some say it is the ghost of a wicked man who sold his soul to the Devil. The story goes that a blacksmith named Will, or perhaps Will O' the Wisp, made a bargain with the Devil for wealth and power. When his time came to pay, he begged for more years. The Devil, in mock mercy, said, "You may live forever, but you will wander the marshes with a lantern, never finding rest, and you will lead other souls astray as you were led astray." Others claim the Will-o'-the-Wisp is a mischievous fairy, a sprite of the marsh who delights in confusing travelers. It appears as a friendly light, a lantern carried by a helpful guide, leading weary wanderers toward what seems like a warm inn or a safe village. But as the traveler follows, the ground grows wetter, the mist thicker, and the light always stays just out of reach. Then, with a flicker, it vanishes, leaving the traveler alone in the darkest part of the bog, surrounded by sucking mud and stagnant water.

In the fen country of Cambridgeshire and Lincolnshire, the locals call it the Lantern Man. They tell of how he stands at the edge of the path on moonless nights, holding his pale lantern aloft. If you call out to him, he will turn and beckon, and if you follow, you will find yourself led further and further from the road, into the heart of the mire. The Lantern Man does not speak; he only guides, leading the curious into depths where the water is cold and the mud is hungry. Many have disappeared into the fens, their only legacy a flickering light seen by the next traveler. The legend of the Will-o'-the-Wisp remains a chilling reminder that in the darkness of the wild, not all that glows is a friendly guide, and some lights are meant to lead us not to safety, but to our doom.
moral: Not all that glows in the darkness is a friendly guide; caution and skepticism are the only shields against the lures of the unknown.
source: English folk tradition - Shakespeare references in King Lear

title: Saint George and the Dragon
summary: A Roman soldier of unwavering faith slays a terrifying dragon to save a princess and leads a city toward a new spiritual awakening.
full_text:
In the days when the Roman Empire still held sway over the known world, there was a knight named George, born to Christian parents in Cappadocia. He rose through the ranks of the Roman army, becoming a tribune, a man of courage and unwavering faith. But it is not for his military service that he is remembered; it is for the legendary encounter that would make him the patron saint of England and a symbol of good triumphing over evil.

The story takes place in the ancient city of Silene, in the province of Libya. Near the city walls lay a vast, stagnant lake, as black as ink and reeking of death. In this lake lived a dragon of such terrible proportions that its very breath poisoned the air. When the dragon emerged from the water, its foul exhalations spread across the land like a plague. Crops withered. Livestock died. People fell ill with a wasting sickness that no physician could cure. The terrified citizens of Silene tried to appease the beast. They first offered it sheep, two each day, but soon the flocks were depleted. When no sheep remained, they began offering their own children. The city council decreed that a lottery would be held, and all the youth of Silene would be entered. Whichever name was drawn would be delivered to the dragon the next morning.

One dreadful day, the lot fell upon the king's daughter. The king, whose name in some versions was King Selius, threw himself at the feet of his people. "Take all my gold, all my silver, everything I own!" he cried. "But spare my daughter!" The people, who had lost their own children, were unmoved. "You made the law yourself, Your Majesty. Your daughter must go." The king begged for eight days' reprieve, and the people granted it. During those eight days, the king and queen wept and prayed. They dressed their daughter in her finest silks, as if she were going to her wedding rather than her death. On the eighth morning, with a heavy heart, the king led his daughter to the edge of the lake.

Just as the dragon emerged from the depths, a thunderous roar of a horse echoed across the plain. Saint George, who had been traveling through the region, saw the princess in distress and the monster's approach. Without a moment's hesitation, he spurred his horse forward, shouting a prayer to the Almighty. The dragon lashed its tail, shattering the earth, and unleashed a torrent of fire. George, shielded by his faith and his armor, dodged the flames and drove his lance, Ascalon, deep into the dragon's throat. The beast let out a final, agonizing scream and collapsed into the mud. The princess, freed from her terror, knelt before the knight in gratitude.

But George did not seek reward. Instead, he used the victory to speak to the people of Silene about the power of faith and the existence of one true God. Moved by his courage and his words, the king and all the citizens of the city were baptized, turning away from their old idols. Saint George rode away, leaving behind a city transformed not only by the death of a monster, but by the birth of a new hope, becoming a timeless symbol of the victory of light over darkness.
moral: Faith and courage can overcome even the most terrifying evil, and true victory lies in leading others toward the light.

title: The Green Children of Woolpit
summary: Two green-skinned children appear from an underground world, leaving behind a land of perpetual twilight for a life in medieval England.
full_text:
In the reign of King Stephen, in the middle of the twelfth century, the villagers of Woolpit in Suffolk, England, witnessed a mystery that has never been fully explained. It was harvest time, and the reapers were working in the fields near the wolf pits from which the village took its name, when they came upon two small children who seemed to have appeared from nowhere.

The children were a boy and a girl, and their skin was green, a deep and unearthly green unlike anything the villagers had ever seen. Their clothes were made of unfamiliar fabric, stitched in a style no one recognized. They spoke a language that sounded like no tongue anyone had ever heard, not English, not French, not Latin. They seemed frightened and confused, and they wept inconsolably, their voices high and strange.

A local knight, Sir Richard de Calne, took the children into his home. He offered them food, but they refused everything set before them—bread, meat, vegetables, fruit. For days they ate nothing, their green faces growing pale with hunger. Finally, someone offered them raw broad beans, and they devoured them eagerly. For months, they would eat nothing else, as if the beans were the only thing that could sustain their alien bodies.

Over time, the children slowly adapted to a normal diet. Their green skin gradually faded to a healthy pink, and they began to learn the language of the villagers. But the boy grew weak and sickly. He seemed unable to adjust to the air and the light of the surface world, and he died shortly after being baptized, his spirit returning to the earth from which he had emerged.

The girl survived and grew into a spirited young woman. She took the name Agnes and became a part of the village community. But as she grew older, she spoke of her homeland with a longing that broke the hearts of those who listened. She described Saint Martin's Land, a place where the sun never shone and the light was like perpetual twilight. Everything there was green—the grass, the trees, the sky. She spoke of a world beneath the earth, where the days were dim and the people were small and green like themselves. One day, while tending their father's cattle, she and her brother had heard the sound of bells ringing. Following the sound, they entered a cave and walked for what felt like hours until they emerged into the bright, warm sunlight of Woolpit. The transition was so jarring that they could not find their way back.

Agnes lived the rest of her life in England, but she always looked toward the earth with a quiet sadness. She never found the cave again, and she spent her final years wondering if the bells of Saint Martin's Land were still ringing in the deep, calling her back to a home she could only remember in her dreams.
moral: True home is not always where we live, but where our spirit first found its place.

title: The Sword in the Stone
summary: After the death of Uther Pendragon, the kingdom of Britain fell into turmoil. Centers on Spirit,  human attempt to understand the soul, afterlife, and the unseen forces that govern existence..
full_text:
After the death of Uther Pendragon, the kingdom of Britain fell into turmoil. There was no crowned king, and every knight with enough men and enough ambition thought himself fit to sit on the throne. Law was broken on every side. The poor were trampled, the fields were burned, and there was no one strong enough to bring the evildoers to justice.

In those dark days, Merlin the prophet came to the Archbishop of Canterbury and took counsel with him. They agreed that all the lords and gentlemen of Britain should ride to London on Christmas Day, and there in the Great Church, they would pray for a sign from heaven. So it was done.

On Christmas morning, as the congregation emerged from mass, they saw a miracle in the churchyard. A great stone, four-square like a marble block, had appeared overnight. Set into it was an anvil of steel a foot high, and driven through the anvil into the stone was a fair sword, naked by the point. Letters of gold were inscribed around the sword, and they read: "Whoso pulleth out this sword of this stone and anvil is rightwise king born of all England."

The archbishop commanded the people to stay within the church and pray, and when mass was finished, all the lords and knights rushed to try their strength. The strongest knights in the realm took turns grasping the hilt, heaving with all their might. But the sword did not move so much as a hair's breadth. It held fast, waiting for the one who was meant to draw it.

The archbishop proclaimed a tournament for New Year's Day, so that the knights could prove their worth and perhaps the chosen king would reveal himself. The jousts were held in a great field outside London. Among the knights who came was Sir Ector, a nobleman of good standing, who brought with him his son Sir Kay, newly knighted, and his foster-son Arthur, who served as Kay's squire.

As they approached the tournament grounds, Sir Kay suddenly realized he had forgotten his sword at his father's lodgings. He called to Arthur. "Ride back and fetch my sword. I cannot joust without it." Arthur spurred his horse and galloped back to the inn, but the door was locked and everyone had gone to the tournament. Frustrated, Arthur thought of the sword in the churchyard. "My brother Kay shall not be without a sword this day," he said.

He rode to the church, tied his horse to the stile, and entered the churchyard. The stone stood alone, with the sword glittering in the winter light. Arthur did not know the inscription, did not know the prophecy, did not know that every knight in the realm had failed to move it. He simply grasped the handle and pulled. The sword slid from the stone as easily as a knife from butter. He took it, mounted his horse, and rode back to Sir Kay.

When Sir Kay saw the sword, he knew immediately what it was. He rode to his father and said, "Sir, I have the sword from the stone. I must be king of this land." But Sir Ector saw the truth. He made Kay swear on the Bible how he had come by the sword. When Kay confessed that Arthur had brought it, Sir Ector turned to his foster-son with tears in his eyes.

"How did you come by this sword?" he asked. Arthur told him the simple truth. Sir Ector then had Arthur replace the sword in the stone and watched him pull it out again, effortlessly. He and Kay knelt before the astonished boy. "Now I understand," said Sir Ector, "that you are the true king. God has chosen you." And so Arthur, who had only been a squire, was crowned King of Britain. The sword he drew that day would one day be known as Excalibur, and the kingdom he would build would be called Camelot.
moral: Destiny reveals the worthy.

title: Excalibur
summary: In the time of kings, there was a boy named Arthur who pulled a sword from a stone—proving he was the rightful ruler of Britain.
full_text:
In the time of kings, there was a boy named Arthur who pulled a sword from a stone—proving he was the rightful ruler of Britain. He became king, but his sword was plain, forged by mortal hands.

In his first great battle, Arthur faced King Pellinore. Their swords clashed—and Arthur's broke beneath him. Pellinore could have struck the killing blow, but Merlin intervened with sleep, saving them both.

You need a true sword,' Merlin said. Come with me.' He led Arthur to a mystical lake, its waters silver, its shores pearl-bright. Upon its waters, a lady rose—dressed in clouds, holding a sword that blazed like fire.

I am the Lady of the Lake,' she spoke. This is Excalibur—the Sword of the Lake. I give it freely, but hear my condition: you must grant me any boon I ask, should I call upon you.'

Arthur agreed. He waded into the waters and took Excalibur from the ladys hand. The sword blazed so bright it lit the darkness like thirty torches. The arm vanished beneath the lake.

But Excalibur's greatest magic was not the blade—it was the scabbard. Wear it, and no wound would bleed. The scabbard is worth ten of the sword,' Merlin warned. Keep it close.'

Arthurs sister, Morgan le Fay, learned magic in Avalon. She coveted power and stole the scabbard, giving Excalibur's blade to her lover, Accolon. In their duel, Arthurs sword broke—he carried only a counterfeit. He knew truth by weight alone.

Morgan cast the scabbard into a lake, drowning its protection. Arthur was doomed.

When Arthur fell wounded from battle with Mordred, his final command was clear: 'Return Excalibur to the Lake. Sir Bedivere carried the sword to the water's edge—but it was so beautiful, so powerful, he could not throw it away.

Twice he hesitated. Twice he hid the sword. The dying king saw the clouds darken, knowing betrayal.

On the third command, Bedivere hurled Excalibur. A white hand rose from the lake, caught the sword, brandished it three times toward heaven—then vanished beneath the waters forever.

Some powers must be earned. Some must be returned. That is the way of magic.
moral: Great power must be used wisely, and some debts must be paid.
source: Based on Thomas Malorys Le Morte d'Arthur (1485), Geoffrey of Monmouths History of the Kings of Britain (1136), and the Vulgate Cycle

title: Merlin
summary: Long before Arthur drew the sword from the stone, before Camelot rose from the plains of Logres, there was Merlin.
full_text:
Long before Arthur drew the sword from the stone, before Camelot rose from the plains of Logres, there was Merlin. His origins were strange and unsettling. He was born of a mortal princess and an incubus, a spirit of darkness, and the clergy feared that he would inherit the evil of his father. But the holy man who baptized the infant saw differently. The child, he said, would be wise beyond measure, a prophet and a wonder-worker whose powers would come from God for the good of the world.

Merlin grew into a man whose eyes seemed to look through the present into the deep currents of time. He saw the past as clearly as the future, and to him, time was a river that flowed both ways. He was an old man before he was young, and a child long after he had grown. It was said that he walked backward through the centuries, carrying the wisdom of ages with him, knowing what would come to pass before it happened and remembering events that had not yet occurred.

The first king who sought Merlin was Vortigern, the usurper who sat uneasily on the throne of Britain. His tower at Dinas Emrys collapsed each night as it was built. Vortigern's magicians told him the foundation must be sprinkled with the blood of a fatherless child. Merlin was brought before the king as that very child. But instead of submitting to sacrifice, Merlin revealed the truth hidden beneath the tower: an underground lake where two dragons slept, a red dragon and a white one, whose battles foretold the struggle between the Britons and the Saxons. Vortigern, humbled, spared the boy and listened to his prophecies.

After Vortigern's fall, Merlin became the counselor of kings. He guided Aurelius Ambrosius and then Uther Pendragon. When Uther desired the beautiful Igraine, wife of the Duke of Cornwall, Merlin used his arts to change the king's appearance so that he could enter Tintagel Castle undiscovered. From that union, Arthur was conceived. Merlin took the child and placed him with Sir Ector, to be raised in obscurity until the time was right. He then engineered the miracle of the sword in the stone, knowing that only Arthur would have the purity to draw it.

When Arthur pulled the sword and became king, Merlin stood beside him as the wisest of counselors. He helped establish the Round Table, where no knight sat above another, and guided the young king through the early wars that unified Britain. He interpreted dreams, revealed treachery before it could strike, and wove enchantments to protect the realm from its enemies. It was Merlin who advised Arthur to seek the Lady of the Lake for Excalibur, and Merlin who foresaw the coming of the Holy Grail.

But Merlin was not without his own weakness. He fell in love with the Lady of the Lake, Nimue, and taught her his secrets. She learned his arts and, fearing his power or perhaps tiring of his company, imprisoned him in a crystal cave or, in some tellings, beneath a great stone. There the great wizard remains, according to legend, sleeping through the ages, waiting for the day when Britain will need him again. Some say he will return when the land is in its darkest hour.

Merlin's story is not one of swords and battles, but of wisdom and prophecy. He was the architect of Arthur's kingdom, the voice that whispered destiny into the ears of kings. Though his magic was vast, his greatest gift was his understanding of the human heart and the long arc of history. He knew that kingdoms rise and fall, but that wisdom endures. And perhaps, one day, when the land calls for its greatest prophet, Merlin will wake again.
moral: Fate cannot be changed.

title: The Holy Grail
summary: At the height of Camelot's glory, on the eve of Pentecost, the knights of the Round Table sat in the great hall at a celebration unlike any other.
full_text:
At the height of Camelot's glory, on the eve of Pentecost, the knights of the Round Table sat in the great hall at a celebration unlike any other. As they waited for a marvel to occur, a sudden clap of thunder shook the castle. A ray of sunlight brighter than any that had ever pierced the windows flooded the hall, and there, floating above the Round Table, appeared the Holy Grail, covered in white samite so radiant that no one could look directly at it. It filled the hall with the most exquisite aromas and placed before each knight the food and drink he most desired. Then it vanished, leaving the knights stunned and yearning.

Sir Gawain, the first to speak, rose and swore an oath: "I will go on the quest of the Holy Grail for a year and a day, and I will not return until I have seen it more clearly." One by one, every knight of the Round Table swore the same. King Arthur, seeing his fellowship about to scatter, wept. "You have undone me," he said. "The greatest fellowship the world has ever known will be broken by this quest."

The knights rode out across Britain, each following his own path. Many failed. Sir Gawain, brave but worldly, could not overcome his pride. Sir Lancelot, the greatest knight of all, was barred from the Grail's full revelation because of his adulterous love for Queen Guinevere. He entered the chamber where the Grail was kept, but a voice told him he could not enter further. He was struck down for three weeks and nearly died.

Only three knights succeeded. The first was Sir Galahad, the son of Lancelot, who had been raised in purity by nuns. From his first day at Camelot, he had sat in the Siege Perilous, the seat at the Round Table that would kill any unworthy knight who dared claim it. Galahad was the perfect knight, untouched by sin, his heart fixed entirely on heaven. He drew a sword from a stone that no other could move, and it was prophesied that he would achieve the Grail.

The second was Sir Perceval, the innocent one, who had been raised in the forest by his mother and knew nothing of knightly ways. His simplicity and faith carried him further than armor ever could. The third was Sir Bors, the only one of the three who would return to Camelot alive. He was flawed but penitent, and his humility earned him a place at the Grail's table.

Their journey led them through enchanted forests and perilous castles, to the court of the Maimed King, whose wound could only be healed by the Grail's presence. At last, in the mystical castle of Corbenic, Galahad was brought before the Holy Vessel. He knelt and received his Savior. Then he prayed that he might leave the world. His wish was granted, and angels bore his soul to heaven. Perceval became a hermit and died in contemplation. Only Bors returned to Camelot to tell the tale.

The Holy Grail was never seen again in Britain. It was taken up to heaven, leaving behind a broken fellowship but also a story of faith, purity, and the search for something holier than earthly glory. The quest for the Grail became the highest ideal of knighthood: to seek, to strive, and to find what lies beyond the reach of swords and crowns.
moral: Purity of heart is required for the sacred.

title: Lady Guinevere
summary: She was said to be the loveliest woman in all the island.. Her name was Guinevere, daughter of King Leodegrance of Cameliard, and when Arthur first heard of her beauty, he knew he must have her as his queen.
full_text:
She was said to be the loveliest woman in all the island. Her name was Guinevere, daughter of King Leodegrance of Cameliard, and when Arthur first heard of her beauty, he knew he must have her as his queen. Merlin warned the king: "This woman will bring you great joy and great sorrow. She will love another, and that love will shatter your kingdom." But Arthur, young and in love, would not listen. He married Guinevere, and she brought with her the Round Table as a dowry, along with a hundred knights.

The kingdom flourished in the early years. Guinevere was gracious and wise, beloved by her subjects. But from the first moment she saw him, her heart was lost. He was Sir Lancelot du Lac, the greatest knight in the world, raised by the Lady of the Lake, peerless in combat and poetry. When he rode to escort Guinevere to her wedding, they traveled for days through forests rich with spring, and in that journey, a love took root that would never be undone.

For years, their love remained a secret. Lancelot fought in Guinevere's name, winning every tournament, defeating every enemy. Arthur trusted them both implicitly. But love cannot stay hidden forever, especially when it burns as fiercely as theirs. The court began to whisper. Sir Agravain and Sir Mordred, Arthur's nephew and son, watched and plotted. One night, they caught Lancelot in the queen's chambers. The lovers fled, but the damage was done.

Arthur, bound by his own laws, condemned Guinevere to be burned at the stake. Lancelot, knowing the king would not relent, rode to the execution with a band of loyal knights. He cut through the guards, killed two of Gawain's brothers in the chaos, and carried Guinevere away to his castle of Joyous Gard. The king's justice demanded war. Arthur laid siege to Lancelot's castle, and the two greatest champions of Britain became enemies.

Pope and church intervened, and Guinevere was returned to Arthur. But Sir Gawain, grieving his brothers, demanded vengeance. The war with Lancelot dragged on. While Arthur was across the sea fighting his former friend, Mordred seized the throne in Britain and claimed Guinevere for himself. The queen, faithful even in her failure, fled to the Tower of London and held out against the usurper.

Arthur returned to face Mordred on the field of Camlann. The two armies clashed in a slaughter from which few emerged. Arthur and Mordred killed each other. When the wounded king was taken to Avalon, Guinevere learned of his death and knew that her love for Lancelot had helped unravel the world they had built. She entered a convent, took the veil, and spent the rest of her days in prayer and penance. When Lancelot came to see her one last time, she refused to look upon him. "We have done enough harm," she said. "Go, and find your peace with God."

Lancelot became a hermit and died not long after. They were buried far apart, but in the stories of Britain, their names are forever linked. Guinevere was not a villain. She was a woman caught between duty and love, and her choices, however flawed, were the choices of a heart that could not deny itself. She was, as Malory wrote, a true lover, and therefore she had a good end.
moral: Passion and duty often conflict.

title: Robin Hood
summary: In the days when King Richard the Lionheart was away fighting in the Crusades and his brother Prince John ruled England with greed and cruelty, a young man from Locksley in Nottinghamshire was forced into the greenwood.
full_text:
In the days when King Richard the Lionheart was away fighting in the Crusades and his brother Prince John ruled England with greed and cruelty, a young man from Locksley in Nottinghamshire was forced into the greenwood. His name was Robin Hood, and he was the finest archer England had ever seen.

The story of his outlawry varies. Some say he killed one of the king's deer to feed his starving family and was declared an outlaw. Others say he slew a band of the king's foresters who mocked his archery and then tried to arrest him for poaching. In the oldest ballad, Robin Hood's Progress to Nottingham, he was but fifteen years old when a wager with fifteen foresters turned to blood, and he fled to the forest with only his bow and his wits.

In Sherwood Forest, Robin found others like himself: men who had been wronged by the unjust laws of Prince John and the corrupt Sheriff of Nottingham. There was Little John, the giant who became Robin's second-in-command after a legendary quarterstaff duel on a narrow bridge. There was Much the Miller's Son, Will Scarlet the fiery one, and Friar Tuck, the jolly monk who could fight as well as he could pray. They lived in the greenwood, sleeping beneath the Major Oak, feasting on venison stolen from the king's deer, and wearing clothes of Lincoln green that blended with the leaves.

Robin's code was simple: he robbed from the rich, especially from corrupt churchmen and nobles who had grown fat on the suffering of the poor, and he gave to those in need. Widows were protected. Poor farmers were fed. Young men unjustly sentenced were rescued from the gallows. He was a thorn in the side of the Sheriff of Nottingham, who tried time and again to capture him. The sheriff once disguised himself and joined the outlaws, but Robin saw through the ruse and escaped. He sent the sheriff home naked on a horse.

Robin's fame spread across the land. He could split a willow wand at a hundred paces. He could shoot an arrow so swiftly that it would pin a man's sleeve to a tree before the man could flinch. He was merciful to those who showed mercy and merciless to those who did not. "I love not the rich who grind the faces of the poor," he would say. "But I love the poor man who works and is honest."

When King Richard returned from the Crusades, he heard of the bold outlaw in Sherwood Forest. Disguised as a monk, the king sought out Robin and shared his meal. Impressed by Robin's honor and generosity, Richard pardoned him and his men, restoring their lands and rights. But in some versions, Robin's end was more tragic. Feeling himself growing weak, he went to Kirklees Priory to be bled by his cousin, the prioress, who betrayed him and let him bleed to death. In his final moments, he raised his horn and blew a last blast through the greenwood. Little John came running, but it was too late. Robin Hood, the prince of outlaws, had shot his last arrow.

His grave lies in Kirklees, beneath a mossy stone. But his spirit lives wherever the poor are oppressed and the rich grow too powerful. He is the eternal rebel, the archer who never misses, the man who stole from the rich and gave to the poor.
moral: Justice may require breaking unjust laws.

title: Maid Marian
summary: Maid Marian was the heart of Sherwood Forest.. While Robin Hood was its soul and Little John its strength, Marian was the pulse that kept the Merry Men alive.
full_text:
Maid Marian was the heart of Sherwood Forest. While Robin Hood was its soul and Little John its strength, Marian was the pulse that kept the Merry Men alive. She was a noblewoman by birth but an outlaw by choice, and she was the only woman who could match Robin Hood with both a bow and a sharp word.

Before she came to Sherwood, Marian was Lady Marian Fitzwalter, the daughter of the Earl of Huntingdon. She was betrothed to Robin of Locksley before he became an outlaw. When Robin was declared an outlaw for poaching the king's deer, their engagement was broken by Marian's father, who feared the king's wrath.

But Marian had no intention of abandoning Robin. She slipped away from her father's castle dressed as a page boy and made her way to Sherwood. When she found Robin's camp, she challenged him to an archery contest. Robin, not recognizing her, accepted. She outshot him three arrows to two before revealing herself. Robin laughed with joy and welcomed her to the band.

In Sherwood, Marian was more than Robin's love. She was the band's strategist and diplomat. She negotiated with the local peasants for supplies, gathered intelligence from the village markets, and kept the Merry Men informed of the Sheriff's movements. When Robin was captured and imprisoned in Nottingham Castle, it was Marian who disguised herself as a washerwoman and smuggled a file and rope to him.

When King Richard returned from the Crusades and pardoned Robin, Marian was at his side at the royal court. But the court was too small for her wild heart. She and Robin returned to Sherwood, where they lived out their days in the greenwood, free and untamed.

Maid Marian is remembered as the first feminist of English folklore — a woman who chose love over comfort, freedom over safety, and the forest over the castle. She proved that a woman could be both a lady and a warrior, both beautiful and deadly.
moral: True love cannot be caged.
source: English Folklore (Arthur of the Swan tradition)

title: Little John
summary: When Robin Hood was about twenty years old and still gathering the men who would become his Merry Men, he grew restless after fourteen days without sport.
full_text:
When Robin Hood was about twenty years old and still gathering the men who would become his Merry Men, he grew restless after fourteen days without sport. He left his companions in the forest and set out alone, striding through the trees until he came to a narrow bridge that crossed a swift-flowing brook. As he stepped onto the bridge, a stranger appeared on the other end.

The stranger was a giant. He stood at least seven feet tall, with limbs like oak branches and shoulders that filled the path. His name was John Little, and he was a man of good mettle, a Yorkshireman through and through, with a strong jaw and eyes that did not flinch. He had no intention of giving way.

"Stand back," Robin called, stringing his bow. "I am Robin Hood, and I will not move for any man."

The stranger laughed, a deep sound like rolling thunder. "And I am John Little, and I yield to no one. It is a coward's trick to stand there with a longbow and threaten me when I have nothing but a staff."

Robin, who prided himself on his courage above all else, tossed his bow aside. "A fair point," he said. He walked to a thicket of trees, cut himself a stout staff of ground-oak, and returned to the bridge, hefting the wood in his hands. "Now we are equal. Whoever falls into the brook loses the fight."

They began a battle that would become legend. Their staffs whistled through the air and cracked against each other like thunder splitting a tree. Robin struck first, a blow that made the giant's bones ring. John answered in kind, and the impact shuddered up Robin's arms. They fought as fiercely as men threshing wheat, neither willing to yield, their feet planted on the narrow bridge. But the stranger was stronger and more patient. He caught Robin on the crown with a crack that drew blood, and then, with a final mighty blow, he sent the outlaw tumbling headfirst into the brook with a great splash.

Robin surfaced, sputtering and gasping, and found the stranger laughing on the bridge above him. "Where are you now, bold Robin?"

Instead of anger, Robin laughed too. He waded to the bank, dripping wet, and blew three notes on his horn. Fifty bowmen burst from the trees, bows drawn. "What is your will?" asked Will Stutely. The stranger braced himself. But Robin held up his hand. "This man has beaten me fairly in combat. He is the bravest soul I have met. John Little, will you join my band and learn to shoot a longbow?"

John Little agreed. Will Stutely, always one for ceremony, announced that a new infant needed christening. They fetched two fat does for the feast and gathered around. "This infant was called John Little," Stutely declared. "But his name shall be changed. We will transpose the words, so wherever he goes, his name shall be Little John." They roared with laughter at the joke, for he was anything but little.

And so the giant was called Little John. They dressed him in Lincoln green and gave him a longbow of yew. From that day forward, he was Robin's right hand, loyal unto death. In the ballads he is the one who rescues Robin from Nottingham prison, who disguises himself as Reynold Greenleaf to trick the sheriff, and who stands by his leader until the very end at Kirklees, where he helped carry Robin to the window so the outlaw could shoot one last arrow to mark his grave. In every story, Little John stands tall, not just in stature but in loyalty, the quiet giant beside the legendary archer.
moral: Strength serves wisdom; loyalty outlasts all.

title: Friar Tuck
summary: Of all Robin Hood's Merry Men, none was more surprising than the holy man who could fight like a soldier and laugh like a drunkard.
full_text:
Of all Robin Hood's Merry Men, none was more surprising than the holy man who could fight like a soldier and laugh like a drunkard. His name was Friar Tuck, and he was a curtal friar, a wandering monk who had left the walls of Fountains Abbey when the life of cloisters proved too narrow for his immense spirit.

Tuck was a jovial mountain of a man, barrel-chested and broad-bellied, with a round face half-hidden by a bristling brown beard and a tonsured crown shining atop his head. His eyes were quick and merry, always one glance away from a wink. He wore a simple brown habit cinched at the waist with a rope, but beneath it he carried a broadsword and a buckler, and he knew how to use both. He was the only man in all of Sherwood Forest who could match Robin's wit as easily as his steel.

Their first meeting was a battle that became legend. Robin had heard rumors of a friar living near Fountains Abbey who could outfight any man in Yorkshire. Swearing an oath never to eat or drink until he found this holy terror, Robin set out with his bow and his pride. He found Tuck walking by a wild river, a stout cudgel in one hand and a sword at his hip, the very picture of a warrior monk. Robin, full of mischief, asked the friar to carry him across the water. Tuck, who knew mischief when he saw it, hoisted Robin onto his back without a word and waded into the swift current. But when they reached the far bank, Tuck drew his sword and demanded that Robin carry him back. The back-and-forth continued until, in the middle of the stream, Tuck flung Robin headlong into the water.

They fought with swords for six hours, neither gaining the advantage, their blades ringing through the forest. Robin blew his horn, and fifty bowmen appeared from the trees. Tuck put his fingers to his lips and whistled, and fifty ferocious bandogs bounded out of the forest, teeth bared. The dogs were trained to attack men, and they would have torn Robin's band apart. "Hold!" called Robin, laughing despite himself. "Stay your dogs, good friar, and let us be friends."

Tuck agreed, and from that day forward he was the Merry Men's chaplain. He heard their confessions, blessed their meals, and fought beside them in every skirmish. He was a man of profound contradictions: a holy man who drank ale from dawn till dusk, a peacemaker who wielded a blade as readily as a cross, a jester who could soothe the most troubled heart with a well-timed jest. He could say mass in the morning and crack a skull in the afternoon, and see no conflict between the two.

He was the one who married Robin and Maid Marian in the greenwood, beneath a canopy of oak leaves, with birds as witnesses and the wind as music. He baptized the children born in the forest and performed last rites for those who fell to the sheriff's men. He was, in many ways, the soul of Sherwood, proof that holiness and joy could be the same thing, that a man of God could also be a man of the people.

In the final days, when the Merry Men scattered, Tuck stayed on in the forest, living as a hermit. Some say he returned to his abbey and lived out his days in peace, a repentant sinner telling no one of his past. Others say he simply walked into the trees one morning and was never seen again. But wherever the story of Robin Hood is told, Friar Tuck is there, juggling, laughing, and showing the world that faith and joy belong together.
moral: Faith finds many paths.

title: Will Scarlet
summary: Will Scarlet was the most hot-tempered of Robin Hood's Merry Men, a man who wore his anger like a cloak and held a grudge like a treasured possession.
full_text:
Will Scarlet was the most hot-tempered of Robin Hood's Merry Men, a man who wore his anger like a cloak and held a grudge like a treasured possession. He was tall and lean, with sharp features and a shock of red hair that gave him his name, and an equally red temper to match.

Before he joined the Merry Men, Will had been a huntsman in the service of a wealthy nobleman. One day, he caught the nobleman's steward beating an old poacher for stealing a single rabbit to feed his starving children. Will intervened, knocking the steward unconscious. For this act of mercy, Will was branded an outlaw and a price was placed on his head.

Will fled to Sherwood Forest, where Robin Hood found him, hungry and defiant, arguing with a group of Robin's men over a deer. Robin was amused by the young man's spirit and invited him to join the band. Will accepted, but only after challenging Robin to a quarterstaff duel, which Will lost — barely.

In Sherwood, Will was both a blessing and a challenge. He was fiercely loyal to the cause of the poor but possessed a sharp tongue that often got him into trouble. He argued with Little John constantly, each man's pride rubbing against the other's like flint on steel. But when a fight broke out, no one fought harder or more fiercely than Will Scarlet.

His finest moment came when the Sheriff of Nottingham ambushed Robin and a small group of the Merry Men. Outnumbered five to one, Will refused to retreat. He stood shoulder to shoulder with Robin, fighting with a fury that made the sheriff's men hesitate. By the time reinforcements arrived, Will had single-handedly held the line.

Later in life, after the Merry Men scattered, Will Scarlet took service with King Richard the Lionheart and fought in the Crusades. He never returned to Sherwood. But his legend lived on — the red-haired hero whose temper was matched only by his courage.
moral: Mercy can heal the angriest heart.

title: The Monkey's Paw
summary: On a cold, wet night at Laburnam Villa, a small house somewhere in the English countryside, the White family gathered around a cheerful fire.
full_text:
On a cold, wet night at Laburnam Villa, a small house somewhere in the English countryside, the White family gathered around a cheerful fire. The blinds were drawn against the darkness outside, and the flames cast dancing shadows across the walls. Mr. White and his son Herbert were playing chess, while Mrs. White sat knitting placidly by the fire. The wind howled outside, rattling the windows, but inside all was warm and comfortable. They were expecting a visitor: Sergeant-Major Morris, an old friend who had spent twenty-one years serving in the British Army in India.

When Morris arrived, the atmosphere of the evening changed. He was a weather-beaten man with stories of exotic lands, and as they sat around the fire, he produced a strange object from his pocket. It was a mummified monkey's paw, dried and shriveled, its fingers curled into a claw. "It had a spell put on it by an old fakir," Morris said, his voice low and serious. "A very holy man. He wanted to show that fate ruled people's lives, and that those who interfered with it did so to their sorrow. He put a spell on it so that three separate men could each have three wishes from it." Morris explained that the first man had used the paw for his three wishes, and the third wish had been for death. That was how Morris had come to possess it.

Morris, who had clearly had a terrible experience with the paw, tried to throw it into the fire. But Mr. White, skeptical and curious, snatched it from the flames. Morris warned him solemnly, but Mr. White insisted on keeping it. After their guest left, the family laughed about the tale. Herbert, the son, joked that they should wish for something practical. "If you only cleared the house, you'd be quite happy, wouldn't you?" he said. "Well, wish for two hundred pounds, then. That'll just do it." Mr. White, amused, held up the paw. "I wish for two hundred pounds," he said. He dropped the paw with a cry, claiming it had moved in his hand like a snake. But nothing else happened, and they went to bed, dismissing the whole affair as nonsense.

The next day, Herbert left for work at the local factory. Later that morning, a solemn man in a black suit arrived at the door—a representative from Herbert's employer. He stammered, his face pale, unable to deliver the news. Finally, he said it: Herbert had been caught in the machinery. He was dead. Killed instantly. The company denied all responsibility, but they wished to offer compensation. They had prepared a sum of money for the bereaved family. The man placed an envelope on the table as the old woman's scream filled the house. Inside the envelope was exactly two hundred pounds.

The Whites buried their son in the new cemetery, two miles away, and returned to a house steeped in shadow and silence. Days passed, heavy and unbearable. Mrs. White grew increasingly distraught, her grief turning to something like madness. One night, a week after the funeral, she woke with a wild idea. "The paw!" she cried. "The monkey's paw! We've only had one wish. We can have another. Go down and get it quickly, and wish our boy alive again!" Mr. White recoiled in horror. "He has been dead ten days," he said. "And besides, I could only recognize him by his clothing. If he was too terrible for you to see then, how now?" But Mrs. White would not be dissuaded. She was frantic, possessed by a desperate hope. "We had the first wish granted," she said. "Why not the second?"

Reluctantly, Mr. White went down to the dark parlor and found the monkey's paw on the mantelpiece. His hand trembled as he held it. A horrible fear seized him—that the unspoken wish might bring his mutilated son before him before he could escape. He raised his hand and whispered, "I wish my son alive again." The paw fell to the floor. He sank trembling into a chair as his wife, with burning eyes, went to the window and raised the blind. Nothing happened. The street outside was quiet and deserted. Mr. White felt an unspeakable sense of relief. The talisman had failed. He crept back to bed.

But then, in the dead of night, there came a knock. It was a soft, hesitant knock at the front door. Mr. White sat up in bed, his blood running cold. Mrs. White, her face wild with joy, rushed to the stairs. "It's Herbert!" she screamed. "It's my boy!" A second knock, louder this time. She stumbled down the stairs, fumbling with the locks. Mr. White followed, desperate to stop her. "For God's sake, don't let it in!" he cried. But she was already pulling back the bolt. A perfect fusillade of knocks echoed through the house. Mr. White, on his hands and knees, searched wildly for the monkey's paw on the dark floor. He heard the bolt creaking as it slid back. At the same moment, he found the paw. He frantically breathed his third and last wish.

The knocking ceased suddenly, although the echoes of it still hung in the air. The chair was drawn back. The door opened. A cold wind rushed up the staircase, and from below came a long, loud wail of disappointment and misery from his wife. Mr. White ran down to her side and then to the gate beyond. The street lamp flickering opposite shone on a quiet and deserted road. There was nothing there. Or perhaps there was nothing there anymore. W.W. Jacobs's masterpiece of horror reminds us that fate is not to be tampered with, that the universe has a balance that our wishes cannot disturb, and that sometimes, the most terrible thing of all is to get what we ask for.
moral: Careful wishes.
